desc:
This passage is quite narrow and not very high. The ceiling has partially fallen
down in a number of places leaving rocks and earth blocking parts of the
passage. Slime covers the damp northern wall and occasional sounds of distant
crashing waves can be heard.
name: A Damp Passage

desc:
This hole is very cramped and dark with only a small opening upwards. The
chamber consists simply of a hollowed out sand pit, though exactly how the pit
was created is not evident. There are a few rotten wood planks visible along the
sides of the pit suggesting that at one time it may have been used as a storage
facility. Below some planks to the north, a small opening is visible.
name: In a Dark Hole

desc:
Sand has piled up in various areas here, creating an uneven ground. The sounds
of a not too far off body of water reach here from the west. Aside from the
mounds of sand, there is little change in the landscape. A small building can be
entered directly east.
name: Sandy Beach

desc:
Though most of this field consists of grass bushels, the sand underneath is
quite visible. Trees have apparently found it difficult to establish themselves
here due to the soft sand not providing much support. The field becomes sandier
as it leads westwards to the sea. An enclosed section of sand lies to the east.
name: A Sandy Field

desc:
You are just inside the city walls of Harlond, the part of Grey Havens on the
southern shore of the Gulf of Lhun. A main road heads west and a smaller gravel
road leads north and south. Through the gate to the east lies outdoor adventure
and possibly danger.
name: Inside the Grey Havens

desc:
The gravel road leads north and south. To the west is what looks like a small
barracks. A ladder with stone steps has been carved out of the city wall here.
It leads to a small room that sits atop the city wall. Along the base of the
wall are some small plants and bushes.
name: Rampart Road

desc:
Rampart Road continues north and south here, and to the west you see a small
cobblestone street. Along the base of the wall grow some small plants and
bushes.
name: Rampart Road

desc:
The road leads north-south, following the city wall that separates The Grey
Havens from the wilderness. To the north a small watchtower rests on top of the
city wall, looming high above this road. Some small plants and bushes grow along
the base of the wall, seemingly undisturbed by the immense traffic.
name: Rampart Road

desc:
The road stops here against the great city wall to the south. A number of bushes
grow here along the base of the wall and you can see some roses. A circular
staircase rises up to a tower on top of the city wall here.
name: Rampart Road

desc:
A small trail runs between large rocks and wild rose bushes. To the west a path
follows the city wall and continues towards the shore of the Gulf of Lhun. To
the east the north-east corner of the city wall can be seen with a staircase
leading up to the top of it.
name: Path along the City Wall

desc:
This large area has been planted with many fruit trees. You just might be able
to find something to eat here. Off to the east is the city wall and a small
cobblestone street lies west.
name: Orchard

desc:
You stand on a small street that does not look too heavily travelled. Small
wooden houses line both sides of the street, and elven children play happily in
the small yards. The street leads north and south from here and a pretty home to
the west has a large sign above in front of it.
name: Small Cobblestone Street

desc:
The street ends here with the shore directly ahead to the north. Off to the west
is a dense collection of homes almost like a miniature village inside Harlond. A
number of pretty flowers grow here likely planted by nearby residents. The city
wall blocks travel east of here.
name: Small Cobblestone Street

desc:
This sandy area is covered with many large rocks. Moss is growing on many of the
rocks and seaweed carried in by the tide lies everywhere. Many birds crawl
around here looking for small fish unfortunate enough to have been washed
ashore. Travel further east is restricted by the increasing number of large
rocks and the nearby city wall. Off to the south are some elven homes.
name: Rocky Shoreline

desc:
This short wooden pier stretches about 200 feet out into the Gulf of Lhun. Many
elven fishermen sit here for hours trying to catch something for dinner. The
waves crash against the pier and it is not uncommon to get drenched as you fish
here. The winds in the Gulf are strong and at times even the hardiest fisherman
must head for cover.
name: Fishing Pier

desc:
The sandy shore ends here at the beginning of a small cliff. The cliff is not
high but it is steep enough that you have no chance of climbing it. The rocky
surface is smooth but bumpy. At one time it probably had sharp edges but erosion
by water has taken its toll. The rock face is covered with bright green moss.
Many strong waves crash against the rocks and water splashes in your direction.
There are many crabs crawling around here. They seem to like the shade offered
by the rock face.
name: Rocky Crag

desc:
This short slightly sandy road heads north towards the shore. Rows of elm trees
have been planted along it and it is quite pretty. Up ahead you can see the deep
blue water of the Gulf of Lhun. Violent waves crash against the shore and many
birds fly above you.
name: Sandy Lane

desc:
This is the house of an old elven sorcerer and his wife. The decor of the room
leaves no question that he has been very successful. You can still see numerous
magical tomes and small pouches likely filled with components needed for
powerful magic. Perhaps you will be able to learn some minor skills here.
name: Home of the Old Elven Mage

desc:
This gravel road leads north and south, following the great wall surrounding the
city. Looking up, you see the top of the wall, some twenty feet high, and the
blue sky above it. Some small plants and bushes grow against the base of the
wall here. The road goes north towards the city gate and south towards a large
park.
name: Rampart Road

desc:
This is a large park which seems very popular among the elven citizens. The
trees are fairly dense here and you can see some small animals scurrying about.
The park continues to the south and a small home lies to the west. The solid
city wall lies directly to your east.
name: Havens Park

desc:
You stand in a large, tranquil area of the park with many pretty trees and small
bushes. This is definitely a great place to relax and many elves seem to be
doing just that. The park continues to the west and north and the city wall lies
to the south and east.
name: Havens Park

desc:
You stand in a large, tranquil area of the park surrounded almost completely by
trees. It is so peaceful here that you can easily forget that you are in the
midst of a great city. Several elves lounge here and there, resting against the
trunks of the great trees. The park seems to continue to the east and there is a
small home to the north.
name: Havens Park

desc:
You have entered a small house situated among some small young trees. This
peaceful place houses a ranger quite willing to teach you all his skills. With
the park and some fairly dense areas of trees to the south, he easily has space
to teach you skills like riding and tracking.
name: Ranger's Home

desc:
You are now in a decent sized building with many well-stocked shelves. Many
people are moving around in here and the grocer's business seems to be doing
pretty well.
name: Grocery

desc:
The road runs north and south here. Off to the north is a large square which
appears quite populated. The road here is lined with many buildings but none
have entrances from here.
name: Main Road

desc:
This is the busiest road in the city. It runs west and east from here, and
people are hurrying along in both directions. From the north you can smell the
aroma of fresh-baked bread, and to the south you see what looks like a small
grocery.
name: Main Road

desc:
You are on the busiest road in town, leading north and south. To the west you
can see a grassy area, with several people walking here and there and children
playing among the trees. You are barely able to avoid being pushed and shoved by
the groups of passing people.
name: Main Road

desc:
You stand on the Main Road, which leads to the north and west. To the east,
barely noticeable, is a small street heavily shaded by monstrous trees, and to
the south you see a large grassy area. To the south is a beautiful house with a
highly decorated door.
name: Main Road

desc:
You can walk in virtually every direction here. To the east and west the Main
Road continues, and to the north you see a small grassy area. A much larger area
of greenery is to the south and it seems to extend for quite some distance.
name: Main Road

desc:
This is a small courier post, much like a post office. This particular post
serves Harlond. Elves in travelling clothes sprint here and there awaiting the
daily rider to arrive. There is a small sign posted on the wall here.
name: The Courier Service

desc:
You have entered Kevlar's armour shop which specializes in leather and chain
mail. Although this is a small shop, the items are of fine elven quality. The
wall behind the counter is covered by shiny pieces for sale and the heat from
the forge can be felt coming from a back room.
name: Kevlar's Fine Armour

desc:
You have entered the yard dedicated to the teaching of warrior skills. Warriors
of all types and ability fence and practice other skills all around the yard,
and the smell of sweat is almost overwhelming.
name: Warriors' Guild

desc:
You are on a small street leading north and south. Small houses line the eastern
side of the street, and to the west you see a white building with large pillars
of stone standing beside the entrance. To the south you hear some splashing
sounds.
name: Small Cobblestone Street

desc:
This is probably the biggest bakery in the town of Harlond. The selection of
fresh goods is enormous, and the prices seem quite reasonable.
name: Bakery

desc:
The Road continues on to the south and to the west where it passes through a
gate to the older part of the town. To the east you see a small grassy area, and
to the north a smaller street leads to a row of buildings.
name: Main Road

desc:
The older, but richly ornamented facades reveal that this is an older part of
Grey Havens. To the east, newer houses can be seen through a gate, and this
street continues to the west, sloping slightly downwards.
name: Valinor Street

desc:
The nets here are not fishing-tackle, but real cobwebs. The elves have long
appreciated the beauty in the skilfully woven webs glistening with morning dew
and this is a garden where the not completely tame spiders have been encouraged
to freely show their art. Less natural webs can be seen to the west.
name: Spider Garden

desc:
This is the main construction place for ships in Harlond. Half-built ships rest
here like the ribcage of a dead animal. Singing ship-builders and woodworkers
are here building the hull of the famous elven ships. They treat the wood with
such care and love you would think it was alive. Cirdan himself often directs
the building helping keep them as sea-worthy as they have been the last
half-dozen millennia.
name: Shipyard

desc:
This small street runs south between a few trees and north towards a large
shipyard. There are no buildings around here, only trees with thick, brushy
undergrowth. It looks like a park that is nurtured to grow wildly, maybe to
remind the citizens of The Grey Havens of the wilderness outside their city
walls.
name: Shady Street

desc:
You stand on a small north south street. Many trees shade this road and the air
is a bit cool. A large decorated square lies to the south and a large shipyard
lies north. Off to the west barely visible through the trees is a small
building.
name: Shady Street

desc:
You stand in an ornately decorated square in the middle of the city. Many people
relax here after a hard day's work and listen to the music of one of the many
travelling bards who make a point to stop here. To the west and east you can see
the two parts of a delightful garden.
name: Starlight Square

desc:
The street continues north and south, running between buildings of all shapes
and sizes. To the east, there is a small building, with the sounds of yelling,
shouting, and clanging steel coming from within. To the west a narrower lane
sneaks away out of sight.
name: Central Street

desc:
This is Central Street, which leads north and south. To the east, the clanging
of metal suggests a blacksmith selling hard armour of high quality, and you can
buy softer garments to the west.
name: Central Street

desc:
This is the beginning of Central Street. It continues north leading between many
large and small buildings. To the west is a small park and to the east is a
large white building.
name: Central Street

desc:
You have entered a small out of the way park. The grass is well kept as are the
bushes and trees that are scattered here and there. There are small benches
placed under the shady trees in several spots and many elves come here for peace
and quiet.
name: Grassy Park

desc:
This shop is very finely furnished. Exotic clothing and materials lie here and
there, hung on pegs and neatly folded on tables. A wide selection of more
mundane and utilitarian clothing is also available, for those who enjoy a more
rugged lifestyle.
name: Tailor's Shop

desc:
A narrow, sneaky alley this is, but it is not at all unfriendly. Neighbours are
talking loudly to each other from one window to another across the street and
elven children are running around playing hide-and-seek in the backyards and
obscure twists of the street. The alley goes east-west here.
name: Crab Alley

desc:
Solid wooden beams criss-cross the angled ceiling of this airy comfortable
house. A few narrow but equally strong wooden columns support the roof. During
the day, light streams through the many round skylights. During the night, brass
lanterns hanging from the ceiling beams come alight. Rows of spacious wooden
shelves line the walls, filled with all manners of hooks, ropes, nets, strings,
wooden and metal pieces, and a variety of hunting equipment.
name: Hunter's Guild

desc:
This lovely area surrounded by a tall gate gives Cirdan a place to come and
relax when he wants to be alone. In the centre of the room is a natural spring
turned into a private bath large enough for several people to comfortably rest
in. Since the water is constantly cycling through it is quite drinkable and very
relaxing. Throughout the area are numerous trees of all types and some small
bushes with pretty white and yellow flowers.
name: Cirdan's Private Spring

desc:
This beautiful house is richly decorated with paintings and colourful
tapestries. A large fireplace is at the western end of the main room. Several
smaller rooms are connected to this large one but they don't have the appeal
that this main room does. A large oak table lies close to the centre of the room
and it could easily seat 15 people. Through a gate on the eastern wall you can
see a small wooded area surrounded by a tall fence.
name: Cirdan's Home

desc:
The large dining area feeds the town guards. Numerous long tables stretch out
from east to west here. Several small adjoining rooms are obviously used for
preparing the food served here. You can smell the wonderful aromas of fresh
cooked food in the air. It appears that there is some left over food here. Maybe
you can take some. There are exits north and west from here.
name: Guard's Mess Hall

desc:
This large undecorated building houses many of Harlond's elven guards. It is
sectioned off so each guard has a small private area. Most of the guards must be
about in the city right now since only one is here. There is a large table in
the centre of the room and a fireplace on the southern wall. The guard glances
in your direction, wondering whether you need assistance. The building continues
to the east.
name: Guard's Quarters

desc:
You stand in a dense grove of trees. It doesn't really surprise you to find a
grove this dense in the city, since elves love trees and prefer to live among
them. This is as close to the wilderness as the elves in the Grey Havens can
get. A path heads north to the road and there is a gate to the west.
name: A Dense Grove

desc:
You have entered a large fenced in area. Some of the elves around here have
started growing grapes. You can see many thick grape vines covered with plump
and juicy purple grapes. The plants are well-pruned and you wouldn't be
surprised if a great deal of wine is produced in Grey Havens simply because of
this vineyard. There is wooden sign planted in the ground here.
name: Vineyard

desc:
You now stand in the small Harlond library. This is only a smaller branch of the
main library which lies across the Gulf in Forlond. Many of the books here are
handmade copies while the originals lie in Forlond. Still there is a great deal
of interesting reading here about the history of the elves and their interaction
with the other races. The only exit is to the east.
name: Small Library

desc:
This small enclosed room has been built over the wall near the Harlond gate.
Narrow slits in the wall allow the elven guards to see when someone or something
is approaching the town. It is very crowded in here and the guard wishes you
would leave so he can get back to his watch even though he does not expect to
see anything dangerous in these peaceful times.
name: Observation Post

desc:
You are now on top of the city wall which stretches far in each direction. From
here archers were able to help defend the city. At the eastern edge of the wall
is a barricade which has holes the elves could aim through. Thus they were
partially protected from arrows shot up from below. It is fairly dusty here now
and there are some old arrows still lying here. It appears that it has not been
used recently and that is certainly a good sign. Far to the east you are able to
see the hills of the Far Downs where many other elves reside.
name: Atop the City Wall

desc:
This is a section of the main road, with a small fountain built directly in the
middle of the street! The water looks clean and refreshing, and it seems that
the children in the city think so too. Many elven children play in the water,
and splash others as they hurry up and down the street. To the north is a
narrower street and the main road continues east and south.
name: Fountain Square

desc:
Large maps, showing the city divided by many dotted lines, cover the walls.
Within each of the divisions are scrawled names of the current occupants or
owners. A large ornate table, almost filling this small office completely,
stands in the centre of the room surrounded by half a dozen equally ornate
wood-carved chairs. Most of the table's top is obscured by papers.
name: Deeds and Apportions

desc:
This large hall is the sanctuary of Doyok, a noble elf who from the look of
things spends his time here sitting in the large lazy chair facing the window.
Through the window lies a large vineyard. Fine elvish wine bottles lay about,
ranging from current vintage to those dating back to Grey Haven's glory days.
Next to the chair on a small table is a well-used clay pipe.
name: Doyok's Sanctuary

desc:
A single rusty door leads into this dark small hall covered in dust. The amount
of dust, and the disheveled appearance of the few pieces of furniture in the
room, might lead one to believe this place was abandoned if not for the trails
of footsteps leading toward a stairway at the rear. A small bed, almost hidden
by the stairway, seems to be the only other thing that has seen any recent use.
A faint shimmering light seems to be coming from the room above.
name: Dusty Hall

desc:
This dimly lit tower looks well organised, a stark contrast to the state of the
room below. Looking around one could see many items that look to be used in the
magical arts, as well as a table in the centre of the room upon which several
weapons have been set. Some of the weapons have a soft glow about them; while
others look like they lay in wait for the same treatment.
name: Tower Workshop

desc:
This resembles more a beach than a field, but occasional hardy plants have grown
strong enough roots to withstand the beating winds from the west. The rear of a
wooden building stands directly east, but there are no entrances visible from
here. The soft sand is covered with tracks from a variety of small creatures
that have dragged themselves across the sand.
name: A Sandy Field

desc:
This small but beautiful temple is often visited by the elven residents of this
part of Harlond. They come here to relax and to be reminded of the wondrous
Valar who await them in Valinor. It is also used by many young elves who wish to
enter the healing profession since some minor skills are taught by the priest
here.
name: Small Temple

desc:
Low, bushy vegetation forms a belt between sandy plains to the north and green
fields to the south. The northern part of this belt is sparser where the sand
begins to prevail. As the belt approaches the fields to the south, the bushes
thrive in fertile soil where they cluster densely, interwoven with high grass.
name: Shrubbery

desc:
The soil is thin and sandy, but still supports an abundance of grass. The blades
grow tall but thin, their yellow-green stalks rising nearly two feet and masking
imperfections in the ground beneath their cover. The soil is richer to the
south, and the grass becomes greener and healthier, but suffers for it as well,
being more heavily grazed by passing animals.
name: Green Meadow

desc:
Very little soil covers the ground, exposing large sections of dark-grey rock.
Thin grass and weeds cling to the soil that has accumulated in cracks in the
stone, while patches of pale-green moss grow in the tiny areas of shade these
plants provide. The land drops off to the west, plunging down nearly fifty feet
to the grasslands below. Despite the steep drop and loose shale, a descent may
be possible.
name: Top of a Low Cliff

desc:
Extensive flat sheets of dark stone cover the landscape, buried in only a few
places by soil and vegetation. A few spruce and fir trees have managed to sink
their roots into cracks and now provide shade for emerald moss, forming islands
of green on a sea of grey. The land drops off into a sheer cliff to the west,
dropping one hundred feet to the grasslands below.
name: Precipitous Drop

desc:
A low cliff drops off to the west, descending to an area of grasslands below.
Near the edge of the cliff, a few large sections of flat stone are exposed, but
everywhere else soil has accumulated atop the rock and now low grass and weeds
cover the landscape. The cliff is not vertical, and a descent may be possible
despite its loose, wet shale surface.
name: Atop a Low Cliff

desc:
The land slopes quickly downward from east to west. The soil is dark and rich,
broken in only a few places by small stony patches, and bears a dense layer of
broad-bladed grass. This green carpet is adorned by white and orange blossoms of
snowdrops and poppies. A few blackberry bushes line the top edge of a ravine
that lies just to the south, its brush-filled depths blocking any travel in that
direction.
name: Sloping Grasslands

desc:
Low grass covers most of the area, providing a green backdrop for the thousands
of wildflowers that grow here. Yellow anemones and hawksbeard, purple blooms of
monkshood and butterwort, and the pale pink blossoms of knotweed all vie for
dominance in this meadow. The flowers largely vanish to the north, but to the
south they seem to replace the grass entirely.
name: Wild Flowers

desc:
This garden is ablaze with colour at any time of year, its occupants carefully
chosen to provide a constantly changing display. Lilies, marigolds, amaranths,
orchids, toadflax, and a hundred other species have been meticulously planted
and tended. Narrow paths lined with bright white stones weave back and forth
between the plants. A small house sits just to the south. A fence blocks the way
to the east.
name: Carefully-tended Garden

desc:
A small copse of trees stands here, surrounded on all sides by low yellow-green
grass. It is dominated by maples, but a few beech and birch trees are visible,
and even a couple of elms. The trees are widely spaced, and provide no trouble
to movement. There is a distinct lack of fallen branches, indicating that they
may be collected by someone nearby for firewood. A small house is just to the
north.
name: Small Patch of Trees

desc:
Extending for miles in most directions, luscious grass carpets this featureless
plain. There are signs aplenty of grazing horses in this fertile field. Perhaps
domestic varieties wander to and from a pasture to the east, or their untamed
cousins come here from the surrounding wilderness. Hidden within the carpet of
grass are numerous burrows, home to various field rodents. A small copse of
trees grows to the west, surrounded by fields.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
Golden brown stalks of wheat spring up from tilled brown soil. This vast field
has been well-tended, and a bountiful harvest could feed an entire village.
Dense bushes run along the north end of the field. Some of them are uprooted and
lie in piles. Gaps in the field shows where a farmer has removed the encroaching
vegetation.
name: Wheat Field

desc:
This field grows tall, green grass which is later harvested and dried to be used
as hay for livestock. A large barn to the east seems to be the most likely
storage place for the hay. A tall fence encloses the field to the west and the
south. A small farm house stands behind the barn and another group of buildings
is visible further away to the west.
name: Hay Field behind a Barn

desc:
This hill rises slightly above most of the surrounding lands, but not high
enough to provide a good view of the area. The soil is studded with small rocks
and a few boulders thrust up out of the ground. Grass is scarce, replaced by
weeds and wildflowers, along with a few thin bushes and stunted trees. A larger
stand of trees is visible to the north. The hill ends abruptly to the south,
where it encounters a sheer rock wall.
name: Low Hill

desc:
Many large boulders break the surface of the ground, along with countless small
rocks. Most of the stones are medium to dark grey, but some traces of brown and
even orange are visible. Thistles and other small weeds thrive here despite the
poor soil. The ground rises to the east, where it peaks at a small hilltop. A
narrow, brush-filled ravine prevents travel to the north.
name: Rocky Field

desc:
The soil is pale brown, laden with sand, and broken in many places by large
upthrusts of rock. A few small patches of grass cling to the soil, but in most
places weeds run rampant. While thistles and dandelions seem to dominate, there
are dozens of other species vying for soil and sunlight. The ground drops off
into a narrow ravine to the north, its brush-filled depths blocking any hope of
travel in that direction.
name: Field of Weeds

desc:
This field of grass and wildflowers rises and falls in tiny hillocks. For the
most part, it slopes downward from east to west, dropping from a small plateau
into broad lowlands. A few scattered bushes rise up above the grass, becoming
denser to the northeast at the base of a narrow ravine. One large patch of
ground, some thirty yards across, is rocky and almost bare of growth.
name: Sloping Field

desc:
Reaching well above five feet, the grass on this plain grows wild and verdant.
Patches of wheat sprout where the wind deposited grains picked up from farmlands
in the distant north. The soil is loose, and families of field mice make their
homes in tiny burrows hidden beneath the grass. Fox tracks are visible around
some of these burrows.
name: Wild Grasses

desc:
This small house has several large windows, none of them containing glass. Thin
wooden shutters stand open, allowing every trace of wind and natural light to
enter the room. It appears the house's owner is not bothered at all by the
elements. A narrow bed rests against the east wall, while a stove and wooden
counter stand against the south. A large loom dominates the centre of the room.
name: Airy House

desc:
This dimly lit barn is filled with the scent of livestock. The livestock is kept
in rows of stalls, each of which is large enough for a single animal. Each stall
contains a pile of straw for sleeping, a trough for drinking, and a bin for the
hay these creatures consume during the winter months. In a corner, manure has
been collected for use as fertilizer.
name: Barn

desc:
Dense grass, much of it over five feet tall, covers this field. The ground is
uneven and marked with long grooves. Standalone boulders lie here and there. A
few bushes grow around these large boulders, making for ideal dens and nests for
small animals. A dry stone wall stands to the south, separating these wild
fields from the pastures it encloses.
name: Wild, Grassy Fields

desc:
Suddenly ending at a sheer escarpment dropping away to the west and north, these
exposed grasslands offer little shelter. The grasses themselves are low and
stunted, grimly clinging to life in the hard soil. A pile of animal bones lies
near the edge of the low cliff, and many skulls can be seen.
name: Atop a Low Ridge

desc:
The plains here are nestled against a long, low ridge to the east and south. The
grass is long and thick, dotted here and there by low shrubs that appear to
provide shelter for birds and other small creatures. At the apex of the ridge, a
narrow stone tower rises above the plains and hills.
name: Sheltered Grassland

desc:
The land is wild here, with thick grasses and patches of scrub growing to the
height of a man's waist. The ground beneath is rocky and uneven, with scattered
larger stones. There is no sign of that anyone has tried to civilise this land
in many years, if ever. A single pine tree rises above the land, its trunk bent
toward the east.
name: Wild Plains

desc:
A carefully-tended field of wheat grows here, its long brown stalks weaving an
intricate dance in even the slightest breeze. The ground below is dark brown,
nearly black, rich with loam and excellent for nourishing these crops. The field
stretches to the north, but seems to be untended there. A small farmhouse sits
just to the west.
name: Field of Wheat

desc:
The ground slopes quickly down from east to west. Hundreds of widely-scattered
bushes dot the landscape, including wild rose bushes, sage, and honeysuckle,
filling the air with their scent. A narrow ravine blocks the way to the south,
its depths filled with dense, thorny brush, while just to the west a cultivated
field is visible.
name: Scattered Brush

desc:
A low shale rockface rises to the east, ascending nearly fifty feet at its
highest, but dropping off to the south. Trickles of water squeeze out from the
layers of rock, merging to form a tiny rivulet that gathers into a small pool
before disappearing into the grass. Moss clings to the rocks, feasting on the
moisture and its abundant load of minerals.
name: Natural Spring

desc:
The land slopes down into a shallow bowl here, framed on the east by a towering
cliff that rises nearly one hundred feet above the ground. The cliffside is
composed of shale, its dark-grey stone laid down in layers aeons ago. The rock
is angled, rising to the north. The top of the cliff sticks out several feet,
threatening to fall at any time. Several deep crevices cut into the rockface,
splitting the already fragile stone.
name: Beside a Tall Cliff

desc:
A small cliff rises to the east, no more than fifty feet at its highest point
here. The ground slopes upward to the north, until it meets the upper lip of the
cliff. The exposed rockface is mainly shale deposited in flat layers, but some
ancient upthrust has broken it, forming a chevron pointing toward the sky.
Despite the steep angle and the loose shale, it may be possible to ascend the
cliff.
name: Bottom of a Low Cliff

desc:
A pebbled beach rises slowly from a shallow river. The beach is roughly twenty
yards wide; across the river lies a grass-covered plain. Branches and other
debris lie half-hidden under the grass, as the river frequently overflows its
bed here before it flows through jagged plains with steeper beaches to the west.
In several places, animal tracks lead to and from the river as the riverbed is
shallow and easy to ford.
name: Fields by a Pebbled Beach

desc:
The river slows down as the riverbed widens. Large gravel shoals rise above the
river's surface. Some of these sedge-covered islands are surrounded by reeds and
seem to be permanent features of the river; excellent places for birds to build
their nests. It is possible to ford the river if care is taken to avoid deeper
pools. The river's flow quickens to the west where it enters a narrow valley.
name: Shallows in the Amber River

desc:
This beach is covered in smooth pebbles deposited here by the river. Larger
rocks among the pebbles come in many different colours and sizes. Near the
centre of the river, a long shoal covered in sedge rises up to the level of the
beach. Further east, the bank becomes steep and high where the river bends.
name: Beach by a Dark River

desc:
Dry stone walls enclose this small pasture, separating it from the wilder lands
to the north and west. There the ground rises and becomes stony and
unproductive. The grass here was once vigorous but is now almost bald from
over-grazing. In places the ground has become dusty and riddled with hoof marks.
A large pile of hay has been set down near the centre of the pasture; stray
strands have been strewn about as horses have pulled at its contents.
name: Enclosed Paddock

desc:
The terrain drops away to the west below a long ridge that extends northward. To
the south, a small river canyon can be seen, the sheer sides offering no path
down. The lands here are jagged and broken as though they underwent some ancient
upheaval of catastrophic proportions. As it spreads to the east, the ground
levels out into a wide, grassy field.
name: End of the Ridge

desc:
Many rocky gullies cross the grasslands, running east and west. Bushes running
along their edges mark them clearly. The terrain is broken by occasional
boulders resting in the grass, and these too are surrounded by low scrub. A wide
promontory extends out to the west, protruding from a long ridgeline that runs
north and south. A wall of stones separates the plains from an enclosure to the
east.
name: Jagged Plains

desc:
This rocky promontory extends out from the ridge in the east, overlooking the
broken plains. A huge stone formation stands here, with ancient, crumbling
stairs leading upwards carved into its side. Below, strangely regular lines of
stone can be seen amid the grasses, and a tiny goat trail leads down the sheer
face towards them.
name: Promontory above the Ruins

desc:
A few crumbling remains of antediluvian walls can be seen peeking above the soil
here. Grasses grow among and atop them, as though seeking to pull them back
beneath the surface, returning these lands to the wild. No other signs remain of
those who built the walls that once stood here.
name: Ancient Ruins

desc:
Low, tough grass and tall, spiky weeds cover the ground, though in many spots
patches of stony soil or bare rock are visible. The exposed stone is mainly
orange-brown sandstone or grey shale. The ground rises to the east, but drops
off in every other direction. Just to the south, a stream thunders and roars
through a narrow canyon.
name: Wild Plains

desc:
This meadow runs between a wooden fence to the north and a stone wall to the
south. The grass is moderately tall and very lush, its colour a deep emerald
hue. The grass is dotted with thousands of buttercups, daisies, and dandelions,
their yellow blooms looking like golden coins scattered across a carpet of
green.
name: Broad Meadow

desc:
Overall fairly flat, this meadow slopes gradually down from the east to the
west. The soil is thick and rich in many places, but stones break through the
surface in others. Emerald green grass stands a foot or more high, hiding many
stones from sight, and making travel somewhat hazardous for the unwary. Several
miles to the west, the Gulf of Lhun is visible. A tall stone fence blocks the
way to the south.
name: Stony Meadow

desc:
This land once stood as a broad hill, but is now truncated by the river that
flows to the south and west. The water has cut deeply into the hillsides, until
the rock beneath has been exposed, leaving tall cliffs. A field stretches out
from the cliffs, covered in dry yellow knee-high grass. A sturdy stone bridge
has been erected on the western cliff-top, extending across the river.
name: Field above a River

desc:
This bridge is wrought from finely-cut stone, its parts fitted with remarkable
exactness. The stone is a uniform light-grey in colour except for the low walls
that rise on either side, which are white. The bridge describes a low arc from
the top of one cliff-wall to another, providing safe passage over the narrow
rushing river below.
name: Narrow Stone Bridge

desc:
A narrow road paved in cobblestones runs straight east and west through a small
elven village. Most of the stones are old and show years of wear, but a few new
cobbles indicate that they are replaced when necessary. The road comes to a
stone bridge just to the east, while to the west it opens out into the centre of
the village.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
The village road becomes a loop here, running in a circle around a large grassy
area. Two gigantic trees grow near the centre of the field, one a laurel, the
other a yew. Both look like they have stood here since the beginning of time.
The grass around them is neatly trimmed and there is no sign of fallen leaves or
branches. The houses and shops of the village are neatly settled along the edges
of the road.
name: Ancient Trees

desc:
This well-maintained cobblestone road runs due east and west through the elven
village, straight as an arrow. It appears to have been swept recently, as it is
completely free of stray leaves or clutter. To the north, a river empties out
into the Gulf of Lhun, with piers thrusting out into both bodies of water. The
strong smell of fish wafts in from the south.
name: Cobblestone Street

desc:
A blend of coarse and fine sand provides soft, uneven footing, except along the
northern edge of the beach where the rolling waters of the Gulf of Lhun smooth
out the surface and bind it together. The half-built hull of a ship rests upon a
wooden framework, well above the high-water mark. Near it, piles of lumber lie
ready to be added. The skeleton of another ship has been laid out nearby, but
construction has not yet begun on it. A small village stands to the east.
name: Sandy Beach

desc:
Several species of wheat grow unhindered, ranging in colour from dark green to
pale yellow and golden brown. Incredibly, there is no clear evidence that this
field was deliberately planted, although there are signs that it is harvested on
a regular basis. The wheat vanishes to the north, where a beach provides too
little nourishment, and to the south, where it is replaced by bushes.
name: Field of Wheat

desc:
Large thick bushes abound throughout the area, most of them covered in fruit at
various stages of growth. Blackberries and raspberries seem to dominate. A very
faint path winds its way between the patches of growth, bearing primarily north
and south. A particularly dense cluster of thorny growth blocks the way to the
east.
name: Field of Berries

desc:
This public house serves both villagers who flock here for conversation and
travellers who seek a hot meal. Several fine tables fill the room, surrounded by
a mixture of chairs, stools and benches. A fireplace centred in the north wall
supplies heat on cold winter nights and the cheerful flicker of flames all year
round.
name: The Wavecrest Tavern

desc:
Small fields of grain are planted in neat squares. The crops are varied, and
include wheat, oats, and rye, each apparently planted at different times, based
on the extent of their growth. Some of the crops are damaged along the south
edge of the field, evidently grazed upon by wild animals. A tall stone wall has
been erected to the north, separating these fields from the lands beyond. A
small farmhouse sits in front of the wall.
name: Well-tended Farm

desc:
This field is clearly cultivated, as it contains almost nothing but flax. The
plants cover nearly the entire area, tapering off only as they encounter rocky
ground to the east. The plants range from six inches to three feet in height,
and seem to be planted and harvested in rotation. A large gully, strewn with
jagged talus deposits, blocks passage to the east, while a low barn lies to the
north, backed by a stone wall that extends all the way to the gully.
name: Field of Flax

desc:
The ground levels out here until it is nearly flat, with only small rises and
drops visible. Short grass covers most of the area, though in many places the
sandy soil is exposed. Weeds stand tall above the grass, most of them crowned
with flowers in various hues of white, yellow and pale purple. The land rises to
the east, with a shallow defile splitting its surface in twain.
name: Flat Plains

desc:
Small, yet sheer, escarpments rise to the north and south. Though no more than
four times the height of a man at any place, they block passage as well as any
city wall. The long, narrow plain between them is stony, with small clumps of
withered grass clinging wherever they can. Running through the centre of the
defile is an unnaturally straight line of lifeless rock.
name: Shallow Defile

desc:
High grass covers the land, its broad blades bending and rustling in the nearly
constant winds that roll in from the west. The land drops off suddenly to the
west, where the river below has eaten deep into the earth, leaving only a sheer
cliff of dark rock. Faint traces of an old road running through the area are
visible, but it has been long abandoned and overgrown.
name: Field Overlooking a River

desc:
The soil is rich and well-watered, resulting in an explosive growth of emerald
grass. The grass stands two to three feet high in most places, and moving in it
requires some effort. In many places the grass has been crushed or bent aside as
wild animals have moved through, leaving a bewildering maze of trails that join,
split, and cross each other at random.
name: Lush Grasslands

desc:
Several species of grass abound here, covering the landscape in a mixture of
hues ranging from pale yellow to deep green. Only a few wildflowers manage to
raise their heads above the grass, pink and white blossoms standing out in
counterpoint to the green. Broken fragments of shale and small boulders of
sandstone litter the ground. A steep, shale-covered slope rises to the east,
while the land drops into a wide defile to the north.
name: Rocky Grasslands

desc:
This field looks like it was once cultivated, but now lies untended. Wheat and
oats grow in profusion, interspersed with their wild cousins. Many patches have
been heavily grazed by wild animals glad for the high quality fodder to be found
here. The land to the south is still cultivated, and a small farmhouse is
visible.
name: Untended Field

desc:
Several large outcroppings of dark-brown sandstone thrust up through the soil.
Years of erosion have smoothed them and worn away their surfaces layers to the
point where this entire area is lightly coated with coarse sand. Weeds thrive in
the poor soil, freed from competition with more aggressive grasses, which are
almost absent here.
name: Sandy Field

desc:
Tall yellow-green and pale brown grass grows here, but very little of it stands
upright anymore. Long years of strong winds and gales coursing across the open
vastness of the ocean have pounded relentlessly at the vegetation, bending it
almost flat to the ground. Some younger, more resilient deep-green blades poke
up between the taller grass, not yet crushed by the scouring winds.
name: Windswept Field

desc:
A wide field spreads out here, covered in most areas by green and brown grass.
The grass has been cropped low in several spots by grazing animals, and in a few
places the sod has been ripped completely clear from the ground. Several small
outcroppings of brown sandstone poke up through the soil. A sturdy wooden fence
stands to the south, while dense thorny bushes grow in abundance to the north.
name: Grazing Land

desc:
This broad depression lies lower than the land in any direction. As a result, it
has accumulated more than its share of salt carried in from the ocean that
pounds relentless on the shore to the west. Patches of weeds, stunted bushes,
and coarse brown grass cover the land, but in many places the soil is bare, too
salty to nourish life.
name: Salty Lowlands

desc:
Years of ocean spray drifting inland with the wind have left this area covered
in a fine layer of salt. Vegetation is patchy, limited mainly to grasses that
are able to survive in the tainted soil. The land slopes gently upward from west
to east. The distant pounding of waves upon the shore pervades the area.
name: Salty Flats

desc:
A tall, sturdy wooden fence surrounds this place, broken only by a gate in the
north and the door of a house to the east. Low grass grows in most spots, but it
has been heavily grazed and in many places bare soil shows through. A long, low
trough stands beside a small well in the southwest corner, the ground in front
of it trampled into a muddy mess by the hooves of goats.
name: Small Paddock

desc:
This house stands well away from any others, far from the bustle of city life.
Great care must have been taken in its construction, as it appears to be quite
old but is still in excellent condition. A stone hearth dominates the northern
wall. Several finely-carved wooden figures line its mantle. Doors stand in the
centres of the west and east walls.
name: Isolated House

desc:
The ground slopes downward from east to west in a gentle gradient as it works
its way toward the distant waters of the Gulf of Lhun. Other than the slope of
the land, there is little variation in its surface, with only minor undulations
to warp the flat surface. Low grass with broad, grey-green blades covers most of
the land, interspersed with taller wildflowers. A fenced-in pasture lies to the
east.
name: Sloping Field

desc:
The soil is a poor mixture of sand, gravel, and clay. Low weeds gather what
nourishment they can, but there are many bare patches where nothing has been
able to take hold. The distant sounds of waves upon the shore drifts in from the
west, and a distinct tang of salt fills the air. The ground becomes much more
fertile to the east.
name: Stunted Weeds

desc:
Aeons of wind and waves have eroded the stone to the west of here, carrying the
resulting sand to this place and piling it up into countless dunes. The dunes
are covered in most places by low, yellow-green grass that has taken seed and
now helps these masses maintain their shape. The land gives the impression of
rolling waves upon the gulf.
name: Grassy Dunes

desc:
The ground has been scoured nearly bare of soil through years of strong winds
ripping their way across the Gulf of Lhun, exposing the bare rock underneath.
The land slopes strongly down from the northeast to the southwest. A low cliff
lies to the north, its base eroded by the surging waves, but as the shoreline
curves around to the south the cliff vanishes until the land merges seamlessly
with the waters.
name: Rocky Shoreline

desc:
Large rocks thrust up above the surface of the gulf, the waves swirling around
them as they roll in and out. The water is only a few feet deep here most of the
time, and almost vanishes at low tide, leaving isolated pools scattered among
the sand. Broken fronds of seaweed and bleached driftwood are snared by the
rocks and trapped in the pools. The land rises in a stony shore to the east.
name: Tidal Pools

desc:
Large masses of rock rise like small islands all along the shore here. Their
surfaces, worn by centuries of wind and water, are in some places smooth, in
others pocked by uneven erosion. Pale yellow sand surrounds them, providing a
navigable route through the area. Tangles of bleached driftwood and dried
seaweed clearly indicate the high water mark.
name: Rocky Beach

desc:
Like giant stairs beside the ridge to the east, the wilderness here descends
from a rocky promontory northwards to a shallow escarpment above the river that
lies south. Wild weeds grow up among the stones littering the landscape. Several
blocks of stone rest at the base of the ridge. They have been heavily weathered,
but were clearly cut and squared at some point long ago.
name: Between the Ridges

desc:
This huge rock formation stands high above the plains, extending upwards from
the ridge below. The area is flat and almost circular, the sides nearly sheer.
Worn stairs leading down from the lip indicate that this place was not entirely
carved by nature, but the builders have long since disappeared.
name: Atop a Stone Pile

desc:
As the river flows towards the west, it drops into a small gorge between rock
walls, no more than twenty feet high on either side. The water rushes across
layers of worn rock. The grey stone bank to the south appear broken and jagged,
while to the north the rocky wall is worn smooth and sheer, offering little
purchase.
name: River through the Broken Lands

desc:
The ground here is rocky and uneven. Tufts of grass grow between the stones,
seeking the soil that has accumulated in the gaps. Three lonely fir trees dot
the area, sinking their roots deep among the cracks, widely separated from each
other. A narrow river flows past to the north and could be reached by a short
scramble down a broken rocky slope.
name: Rocky Riverbank

desc:
Pale brown soil, heavy in clay and sand, provides limited sustenance to the
myriad weeds and wildflowers that grow here. Thistles seem to dominate, their
purple heads appearing dull against the brighter yellow and orange blooms that
surround them. A narrow stream, only a few feet across, runs from the south to
the north. It crosses a low cliff and plunges to join the river below.
name: Beside a Narrow Stream

desc:
This farmhouse consists of a several rooms along a single corridor. From the
perspective of the front door, there is a main room on the left and a storeroom
with well-stocked shelves to the right. A trap door at the end of the corridor
leads down to a large cellar. The centerpiece of the main room is a large oven
which could be used for either cooking food or heating the farmhouse. A wooden
table and several chairs sit near the stove. At the back of the room are three
beds, a cupboard, and two wardrobes. All rooms other than the cellar are well
lit by windows.
name: Inside a Farmhouse

desc:
Simple in its construction, this crude cellar was dug to store the farm's root
vegetables. The walls and ceiling are supported by sturdy planks and stanchions.
The floor is made of large, flat stones. A set of stairs allows access to a
trapdoor which leads up into a farmhouse.
name: Root Cellar

desc:
This house seems to be typical of those built by elves. It has been carefully
crafted to withstand the wear of decades, if not centuries, before requiring
repairs. The interior is a display of graceful, curving lines and patterns of
light and dark woods. The house is very clean and clearly well-tended. A single
door offers access to the street outside.
name: Well-kept House

desc:
The land rises a short distance above its surroundings. It is not enough to form
a true hill, but it does contain the river to the east, forcing it to turn
towards the north. Trees dot the landscape, standing by themselves or in small
groups, primarily poplars and sweet-gums. A small path runs to the north, where
a clusters of houses can be seen.
name: Low Rise

desc:
Some low, dark-green grass grows here, but for the most part it is entirely
displaced by wildflowers. Dozens of species flourish here, varying in height,
creating a multi-tiered display of vibrant colours. From white and pale yellow
to deep purple and red, the blossoms cover the entire spectrum. The heads of the
flowers shift and dance in the slightest breeze.
name: Field of Dancing Flowers

desc:
Built of thick, sturdy wooden planks, this pier stretches out into the junction
of a wide river and Gulf of Lhun. The blend of waters swirls around the base of
the pier, circling the hardened posts on which it rests. The pier drops down at
its northern end until it is only a foot above the water, allowing easy access
in and out of the river mouth.
name: Broad Pier

desc:
The overpowering smell of fish immediately announces the purpose of this shop.
All of the fish here are fresh, however, without even a hint of any gone bad. A
narrow counter runs across part of the room, while bins holding bait and other
fishing supplies line the walls. A variety of fishing rods and nets hang above
the bins.
name: Fish Shop

desc:
The ground is completely water-logged. Clumps of grass, reeds, and other low
plants provide some footing, but care must be taken not to become mired down in
thick mud. Hundreds of small pools dot the landscape, their water tinted brown
and green by mud and algae, and completely undrinkable. The land becomes firmer
to the west, but to the south the marsh becomes even wetter.
name: Shallow Marsh

desc:
The river makes a wide, sluggish turn to the north as it nears its destination.
A rocky cliff frames the northeast edge of the river, while lowlands extend to
the southwest. The river flows very slowly here, at times seeming to entirely
stop. Tall rushes extend along the south bank, and beyond them a shallow marsh
can be seen.
name: Slow River

desc:
Only isolated islands of green and narrow strips of weeds separate broad pools
of water. Tall, dense grass provides some stability in places, but the safe
paths wind in a maze-like confusion with many dead-ends. Cypress trees rise
above the otherwise flat landscape. The water here is dark, nearly black in
places, a thick organic soup teeming with insects, and undrinkable. Hidden below
its surface is deep, oozing mud.
name: Deep Marsh

desc:
The land is mostly flat, with minor hillocks rising and falling just enough to
create the impression of rolling waves. Many trees grow here, widely separated
from each other for the most part, with only a few small clusters. Dogwoods,
poplars, tulips, and hawthorns seem to dominate. A diverse blend of wildflowers
grow in profusion. To the south, the trees draw closer together to form a small
wooded area.
name: Scattered Trees

desc:
This grove is more than just a cluster of trees, it is almost a small forest
which extends nearly a mile in any direction. The trees growing on the outskirts
of the woods are young and fairly small, containing a mixture of birch, larch,
and maple trees. Towards the centre, the trees change to towering elms and oaks,
rising high above the ground. A large artificial complex has been constructed
among the intertwined branches of the trees, resting nearly thirty feet
overhead.
name: Dense Grove of Trees

desc:
The ground rises up into a low hill here, standing above the surrounding lands.
Large sheets and boulders of rock thrust up above the soil, their grey, brown,
and orange surfaces a striking contrast to the greenery that surrounds them.
Many of the stones are dotted with pale green lichen. The ground drops quickly
to the north into a wet, boggy depression, while a small forest stands just to
the east.
name: Low Hill

desc:
Running down from a low hill to the east, the long slope is covered with lines
of cultivated grapevines. Well-constructed wooden frames provide support for the
vines, and many of them bear fruit. A narrow dirt path extends east from the
field, but vanishes as it ascends the rock-strewn hill. A second hillside,
entirely uncultivated, rises to the west.
name: Sloping Vinyard

desc:
The ground is saturated, making for a very soft and muddy surface. Grass and
weeds hold the ground together in most places, making travel possible, if not
easy. A few scattered cypresses rise above the field, enjoying the moisture and
rich nutrients that this area provides. The mud becomes deeper and softer to the
east and north, blocking travel.
name: Muddy Field

desc:
Several small plots of crops have been planted here, including wheat and oats,
along with a variety of vegetables. Surrounding these plots, wild grasses grow,
but they also appear to be tended, perhaps to prevent them from spreading into
the planted areas. The rush of moving water to the north is quite audible. A
sturdy wall built of rounded rocks stands to the east.
name: Tended Field

desc:
This hill, barely tall enough to merit that name, rises only a short distance
above the surrounding lands. Its slopes carry little soil, and the rocky core of
the hill is exposed. A few stunted fir and spruce trees crown the hilltop,
providing at least a little shelter against the winds that often whip across the
land from the Gulf of Lhun.
name: Low Hill

desc:
The trunks of hundreds of dead trees lie upon the ground, evidently the remains
of what was once a small forest. Most of the trees were violently torn from the
ground, perhaps by a massive storm, their exposed roots long since washed bare
of soil. Now the trees lie at peace, covered in green and brown fungi, slowly
returning to the soil from which they grew.
name: Rotting Trees

desc:
A broad platform has been constructed in a triangular form, using three huge
trees as its supports. Several bowers of woven branches linked together by live
vines provide areas of protection against the elements. A number of simple but
comfortable pallets are scattered below the bowers. A large table surrounded by
stools provides a communal dining and meeting area.
name: Treetop Enclave

desc:
The cave entrance opens out into a large chamber. Jagged rocks form the walls
and ceiling, while sand, dirt, and leaves cover the floor. There are many signs
that this cave is frequently inhabited by some wild animal. Strands and tufts of
coarse brown fur are scattered everywhere, and countless tracks of padded feet
wander in every direction.
name: Small Cave

desc:
The river is quite broad and shallow here as it makes a very wide bend from east
to north. The water appears nearly motionless on the surface, but random leaves
and sticks floating past betray its current. A flat rocky shore runs along the
west bank, centuries of silt and small stones paving an area that is nearly free
of vegetation.
name: Lazy Bend in the River

desc:
A broad, shallow river slowly winds its way through wide, lazy bends towards the
distant Gulf of Lhun. The current is very slow here, and anyone floating down
the river might take hours to make any progress. The banks of the river are
covered in deep grass and reeds, especially to the west, where the edge of the
river merges with a marshy area.
name: Gently-flowing River

desc:
As the river flows northwest toward the gulf, it slices through the edge of a
low hill. Centuries of water have washed away the surface soil and have gouged
deeply into the rock beyond, creating a small cliff that borders the northeast
edge of the river. Thick layers of slate form the surface of the cliff, their
dark-grey surfaces covered in many places by moss.
name: River below a Cliff

desc:
The river flows steadily toward the northwest. It runs through broad, flat
plains which must often be inundated during rainy seasons as the river expands
beyond its low banks. The land has a barely perceptible slope, causing the river
to flow quite slowly here, and its bed is thick with silt.
name: Slow-moving River

desc:
Descending gradually from the wooded foothills of Ered Luin, this wide, calm
river flows westward across a broad coastal plain. Reeds grow upon the north
bank, providing a place for fish and other creatures to shelter and feed. The
south bank is covered in a dense tangle of thorny bush, preventing travel in
that direction.
name: Smoothly-flowing River

desc:
The river rushes between high stone walls on either side, their sheer surfaces
offering no access to the lands beyond. The water courses down over a series of
small cascades and falls, dropping a few feet at a time, swirling around and
over large boulders and across flat sheets of rock. The river widens and calms
as it flows on to the west.
name: Series of Cascades

desc:
As it flows westward through a small gorge, the river here winds its way around
occasional boulders that thrust up above the surface like giants making their
crossing. A large tree, dragged into the river by some storm or flood, forms a
hazard along the north bank, where it has become wedged. To the south, a narrow
ledge rises from the shallows to the top of the sheer bank.
name: Narrowing River

desc:
Many types of bushes grow here, most bearing flowers of some sort, colouring the
land with pale yellow and purple and dull white. A few bushes bear fruit, though
not all of it is edible. Thorny brambles line the edge of a cliff that drops off
to the north, preventing any attempt at reaching the river below. The brush
continues to the west, but vanishes to the east.
name: Dense Brush

desc:
Several species of bushes and brambles grow together, leaving little room to
venture between them. Vines and roots hidden beneath low weeds threaten to trip
unwary travellers, while thorns snatch at clothes and exposed flesh. The brush
is particularly dense to the north, where the land descends to meet a river.
name: Dense Thicket

desc:
The ground is soft, but the soil appears to be only a thin layer over sheets of
rock. In many places the dull grey stone is exposed and plant-life is entirely
absent. A few dozen trees dot the area, huddling in tiny clusters that are
widely separated from each other. Just to the north, a wide river flows slowly
past. A wall built from smoothed rocks blocks the way to the west.
name: Scattered Trees

desc:
Plains sweep in from the east, but vanish as they approach the gulf. The soil
thins until it disappears entirely, leaving heavily-eroded rock exposed. The
land ends abruptly in a tall cliff that drops away into the surging waters of
the Gulf of Lhun. The dull throbbing of waves upon the rocks below continues
ceaselessly. To the west, far across the gulf, a dim purple shadow marks the
opposite shore.
name: High above the Gulf

desc:
The land vanishes in a sheer drop to the west. At the base of the cliff, the
waters of the gulf boil and churn around enormous rocks that have broken away
from the land. A thin mist of salt covers most of the land, but very little
soil, and only a few hardy weeds and stunted bushes cling tenaciously to the
cracks in the rocks.
name: Tall Cliff

desc:
The bottom of the gulf approaches the surface enough to be visible, but it is
still too distant to reach. Enormous beds of seaweed grow in the sandy bottom,
their fronds weaving back and forth in the constant motion of invisible
currents. To the south the water becomes much shallower, but to the east waves
swirl around large rocks before crashing against a tall cliff.
name: Above the Waving Fronds

desc:
This close to the shore, the waters of the gulf are shallow, no more than a
handful of fathoms in most spots. Tidal currents ebb and flow around enormous
submerged rock formations, creating a dangerous zone of roiling waters. Below
the surface, the faint outline of a once-proud sailing vessel can barely be
discerned.
name: Shallow Gulf

desc:
The land is completely submerged by the gulf, but drops off very gradually as it
slopes away from the shore. Beginning at only a foot, even at its deepest the
water is only thirty feet deep, and the seabed is clearly visible. Clusters of
life cling to rocks along the bottom, swirling with colourful plants and fish.
The bottom sinks rapidly just to the west.
name: Gradually Deepening Waters

desc:
The river bursts out from between two confining cliffs and expands into a wide,
shallow stream as it levels out and approaches the Gulf of Lhun. The current is
slow enough that at high tide the waters of the gulf mingle with those of the
river, creating a brackish mixture that supports a blend of both freshwater and
saltwater flora and fauna.
name: Estuary

desc:
The water is too deep to make out anything of the floor of the gulf. Low waves
rise and fall constantly, pushing gradually closer to the shore that is visible
a few miles to the east. Schools of silver fish swirl and dart far below the
surface, but they are too deep and too swift to approach.
name: Gulf of Lhun

desc:
Strong currents battle with one another, constantly rearranging themselves as
one gains dominance over the others. Mixtures of warmer and cooler water rise
and settle to the bottom, while the wind brushes across the surface, adding its
influence to the confusion. The gulf continues in every direction.
name: Swirling Currents

desc:
The surface of the gulf appears to be mainly calm, its flat face given texture
only by the constant roll of low waves. Below the surface, strong currents run
through the deep water and even on top of the water their influence can still be
felt. To the east, a distant cliff is visible, and before it the waves crash and
churn around pillars of unyielding rock.
name: Gulf of Lhun

desc:
The waters of the gulf pound relentlessly against the rocky shore. Over untold
years the waves have worn away the coast, leaving a tall, unscalable cliff. The
waves surge around hundreds of partially-submerged rocks, threatening to batter
anyone caught in their grip. A dark tunnel mouth is partially visible in the
cliff just above the sea level. To the west, the gulf becomes calmer and
stretches out into miles of unbroken water.
name: Pounding Surf

desc:
A short and rough tunnel leads from west to east. The ground is wet and tufts of
seaweed are caught behind sharp rock edges. A number of heavy bars are set into
the rock at the cave entrance to the west. To the east, the tunnel opens up into
a cavern. The walls around the eastern end appear to be smoothed and shaped, and
barely visible runes are inscribed on them.
name: A Damp Tunnel

desc:
The waters of the gulf churn and foam around partially-submerged rocks and pound
against a curving cliff face. The constant roar is deafening, and the swirling
waters and grinding rocks may pose a deadly hazard to anyone foolish enough to
try swimming here. To the west, the gulf becomes deeper and much calmer.
name: Roaring Surf

desc:
A wide cavern was carved out by water leaking down from the surface above and
flowing out through a tunnel to the west. Much of the ceiling is covered by
stalactites, but the floor of the cavern was leveled at some point, and only a
few short stalagmites have started to form. A stone platform dominates the
centre of the cavern and bears a huge stone sarcophagus. A tunnel leads out of
the cavern to the west. The floor is wet, and several strands of seaweed have
been caught behind the edges of the platform.
name: A Damp Cavern with a Sarcophagus

desc:
The bottom of the gulf slopes off quickly and the waters become suddenly deep.
Waves roll continuously across the surface, unhindered in their eternal motion
across the deep. The piercing cries of gulls drift in from the east where, a few
miles distant, the shore is faintly visible.
name: Gulf of Lhun

desc:
The waters of the Gulf of Lhun stretch out in every direction. It is only a few
miles to the eastern shore from here, but the western shore is entirely beyond
sight. Waves undulate constantly across the vast expanse of water, their size
dictated by the strength of the wind as it roars or whispers its way across the
gulf.
name: Rolling Waves

desc:
Deep, blue-green water extends miles in every direction. Far to the north, the
sails of a few ships can be discerned. Some are westward bound toward the far
shore, invisible beyond the horizon, while others sail eastward towards the Grey
Havens. Although the water seems calm, strong currents flow below the surface.
name: Gulf of Lhun

desc:
The waters of the gulf are vast and deep, disturbed only by low waves rolling
across its surface. Miles away, a low cliff can be seen to the east, rising
above the waves. Far, far to the west, a barely visible haze may be the far
shore, or simply an illusion. The gulf stretches out in every direction.
name: Gulf of Lhun

desc:
The river squeezes between rock walls until it reaches its narrowest point for
miles. The walls rise above the river a good thirty feet, and are vertical and
drenched by splashing water. Many large boulders have been exposed by years of
rushing water and, combined with broken branches and snags they have caught,
pose a major hazard to swimmers. Stretching overhead, a well-crafted stone
bridge crosses the span from one wall to the other.
name: Beneath a Stone Bridge

desc:
The land rises up out of the waters in a very gentle slope, creating a broad,
shallow shore. The bottom is almost entirely sand, with only a few rocky areas
surviving years of pounding surf. Waves roll in and out, performing their slow,
eternal dance. To the south, the land emerges from the waters, creating a beach.
To the north and west, the Gulf of Lhun stretches out for miles.
name: Shallow Shoreline

desc:
This pier is much narrower than the one to the east, meeting it at right angles
and running perpendicular to it. The boards on this pier are much thinner, too,
but still provide a solid footing. The pier extends out over the Gulf of Lhun,
standing high above its surface. Clearly it is intended for fishing only, not
for mooring boats.
name: Narrow Pier

desc:
From its origin in the Tower Hills and crossing leagues of plains and lowlands,
the river finally reaches its destination. The fresh water of the river merges
with the salty water of the Gulf of Lhun in a confusion of currents. Seaweed and
tiny fish flourish in the blended water, feasting upon nutrients washed in from
the land.
name: River Mouth

desc:
This ship once sailed proudly across the Gulf of Lhun, but now rests forever in
its depths. Algae, coral, and other sea life cling to the surfaces of the ship,
anchored against the currents and slowly absorbing the wood. The starboard side
of the ship has been cracked and broken, and a large hole allows entrance into
the ship's hold.
name: Sunken Wreck

desc:
The ship's hold is a jumbled mess of crates, barrels, and ropes. Cracked beams
still support the deck above and may survive for years, but will eventually give
way to decay. The hatches to the deck above are swollen shut and forever sealed.
Some of the cargo containers are smashed open, and appear to have once contained
fine cloth and food that is now worthless.
name: Inside a Sunken Ship

desc:
This farmhouse is smaller than many of its ilk, consisting of a single room. A
small hearth is built into the north wall, with a stack of seasoned wood set
nearby, ready to feed it. A narrow table runs along the west wall, clearly used
mainly for cooking, while a smaller, square table sits in the centre of the
house, flanked by two well-carved chairs. A single large bed, covered in a
brightly-coloured quilt, rests in the northeast corner.
name: Small Farmhouse

desc:
A large herb garden grows in the space between the farmhouse and the barn. It is
flanked on the north by a high stone wall. A lower wooden fence to the south
protects it from wild animals while still allowing ample sunlight to fill the
area. A wide variety of herbs have been planted here, including catnip, hyssop,
winter savory, lavender, black and silver mint, parsley, thyme, marjoram, and
rue. Together they fill the air with a symphony of aromas.
name: Herb Garden

desc:
This barn is low but spacious. A number of wooden pens have been built all along
the east wall. Tools for planting and harvesting dominate the northern section
of the barn, while the southwest corner is devoted to drying and storing flax. A
large set of double-doors opens out into the fields to the south, while a much
smaller door, painted white, pierces the west wall.
name: Large Barn

desc:
This small cavern has been carved out of the rock. It is clearly not natural,
being almost square, with rough-cut walls of layered shale. Extensive effort
must have been used to create this chamber, but there is no sign that it has
been used in years. The only sign of furniture is a low shelf that runs along
the south wall, perhaps used as a bed or table. The walls are damp, and the
floor slick with water that has seeped in.
name: Crude Chamber

desc:
The land drops into a very low plateau. The ground is nearly flat, but rises to
the east and south. Tall, thin grass is abundant here, with only a very few
wildflowers adding their colour to the scenery. A sluggish river flows just to
the west, and beyond it the Gulf of Lhun is visible.
name: Low Grasslands

desc:
The plains here are rent by long scars running nearly parallel to each other.
Low, rocky ridges run through the area, resembling nothing so much as a great
confluence of ever dry riverbeds. Giant boulders emerge from the grass like
sleeping beasts waiting through the ages for someone to return and awaken them
once more.
name: Broken Land

desc:
This small building contains a table, chairs, and several bunks. A small
fireplace is set into the western wall, but aside from that, the room has no
decorations of interest. Although most of the guards quarters are further into
town, several stay here close to the gate in case of trouble.
name: Guard Post

desc:
Loose stones cover the floor of this cave, which extends for dozens of yards in
every direction. The rocky walls are flat, but covered with small cracks. The
roof appears to be of an entirely different stone, brown and earthy. Although
clearly well-built at one time, this cave is so ancient that the ages will soon
reclaim it.
name: Broad Cave

desc:
Hidden behind the cave to the west, this ancient room may well have lain here
undisturbed by the winds of time for thousands of years, perhaps even since the
days of Earendil. Shards of broken masonry litter dusty corners, and an ancient
stone chair rests atop a slightly raised dais against the eastern wall. Faint
carvings can be seen on each wall, although what they represent is impossible to
determine.
name: An Ancient Chamber

desc:
A steep slope of crumbly shale descends downwards from a small, jagged opening
that has been covered with uprooted bushes and briars. The slope connects to the
bottom of a mossy cavern which branches into dry, water-worn tunnels that cut
into the shale bedrock. Boot prints litter the dirt in all directions.
name: Hidden Shale Cavern

desc:
A huge cavern that is connected by a network of tunnels has been eroded out of
the shale bedrock here. The ceiling and floor of the cavern are covered in red
coloured stalagmites and stalactites that surround an underground pool. Tracks
lead to other dusty, water-worn tunnels that are large enough to explore.
name: Enormous Shale Cavern

desc:
This tunnel is progressively wetter and muddier as it heads downwards into the
earth. The mud is slippery and littered with deep tracks alongside evidence of
previous travellers slipping. The tunnel opens up into a larger section covered
in strange moss before narrowing.
name: Wet Shale Tunnel

desc:
This tunnel forks into two smaller, erroded passages that abruptly end in
deadends within the shale bedrock. Piles of dusty, sharp rubble hint that there
was a cave-in at some point ages ago. Boot prints litter the ground heading down
the opposite end of the tunnel.
name: Deadend Shale Tunnel

desc:
Bat guano fills the sides of this tunnel in huge piles several feet deep. The
middle of the tunnel is strewn with debris that comes in during floods from a
narrow crack in the ceiling that leads to the outside. The bats seem to be
missing and were most likely disturbed by men whose tracks litter the tunnel.
name: Filthy Shale Tunnel

desc:
The river is wide and almost silent in this place. Its clear waters reveal a bed
of flat stone, flowing lightly over a long series of shelves that drop off only
an inch or two at a time. Thick reeds grow along the north bank, but many have
been trampled by animals coming here to drink. The south bank of the river is
barren of most vegetation, covered instead by broken shale and slate.
name: A Deep River

desc:
You have been thrown into one of Harlond's nice, clean, roomy jail cells. If you
did not know any better, you would think this is a room in an respectable inn.
On the right you see a bed and on the left there is a window where you can look
out over the great city in the day and in the night you can probably see the
rows of street lamps that light up the vast city.
name: Jail Cell
